result:
The night the old man finally fixed the porch light, his wife had already been dead for two years.

He didn't do it because he needed to see the steps. He'd memorized them—three down to the walkway, then the crack that caught your left foot if you weren't paying attention, then fourteen more to the mailbox. He could navigate it blind, which was essentially what he'd been doing all those months, the bulb burned out and nobody coming to visit anyway.

He did it because the darkness was talking to him.

Not in the way people worry about when they think about old men living alone. Not voices. Just the darkness itself had a quality now, a weight that pressed against the glass door and said *this is what it looks like when you stop trying*. Every night he'd sit in the recliner and look out at the black rectangle of the porch and feel it looking back, patient and enormous, and he knew that if he didn't do something it would eventually come in.

So he climbed the stepladder on a Tuesday in October, screwdriver in one hand, the new bulb in the other, and he changed it. When he flipped the switch, the porch materialized. The white railing he hadn't really seen in months. The pair of rocking chairs, both still out there, both still occupied in his mind by people who no longer sat in them.

He stood at the door and looked at what he'd made visible again, and he wept because it was so small. Just a porch. Just a light. Just a little glowing square against the infinite dark. But it was his.

---

That was the year I turned thirty-one, though I didn't know the old man yet. I was living in a city four hundred miles away, working a job that required me to sit in a gray room and explain to people why the things they'd paid for weren't working. Not broken in the sense of *we'll fix it*—broken in the sense of *this is how it works now*. I was good at it, which was the worst part. I could make you feel like the problem was reasonable, just a natural feature of the landscape, and you'd leave the conversation calm, not realizing until much later that nothing had been resolved.

I was learning the craft of explaining away.

At night I'd go home to my apartment, which I'd decorated carefully with things I'd chosen from catalogs—each item individually appealing, together amounting to nothing. A leather chair I never sat in. A bookshelf with books I'd read halfway. A kitchen where I boiled water for tea and then let it cool while I refreshed a screen, again and again, waiting for the thing that would feel like a reason.

I didn't know what the thing was. I still don't, exactly. But I knew I was waiting for it. You can always tell when you're waiting, even if you can't name the arrival point. There's a posture to it, a lean. You're never quite standing on both feet.

---

My mother called on a Thursday to say the old man had died, which was how I learned he existed. He was her father. My grandfather. A fact that had been sitting in the family's invisible storage for decades, alongside other facts nobody mentioned: the year she left home, the reason she didn't go back, the way she'd said *he's not part of this* with such finality that I'd understood the subject was closed before I understood what the subject was.

Now he was dead and she was crying, which confused me because I'd never seen her cry about him before. But grief is strange. It can arrive late, like a letter forwarded too many times, the handwriting on the envelope already obsolete.

She asked me to go. To the house. To handle it. She couldn't, she said, and I understood that *couldn't* was doing the work of several longer sentences.

---

I drove down on a weekend in November. The porch light was on when I arrived, and I didn't know yet that this was remarkable. I just thought: someone left a light on. A house with a light. What you expect.

The key was under a flowerpot, which seemed impossibly old-fashioned, like a detail from a story my mother might have told me if she'd told me stories about him. I turned the lock and the door opened into a smell I recognized without knowing—old paper, wood polish, something like smoke but sweeter. The smell of a house that had been lived in by one person for a long time. A specific kind of alchemy.

I walked through the rooms and tried to see him. The recliner worn in the shape of a body. A coffee mug on the table, ringed with the residue of mornings. A calendar on the wall, still turned to October, the month he'd died. I flipped it to November for him and then felt strange about it, as if I'd come into someone's house and started correcting things.

On the porch, I sat in one of the rockers and looked out at the street. A woman walked a dog. A car passed slowly, its headlights sweeping across the neighbor's yard. The light above me buzzed faintly, keeping the dark at a specific, manageable distance.

I understood, suddenly, why he'd fixed it. Not to see. To mark. To say *this far*. To draw a line in lumens and say *I am here and the dark is there and there is a border between us, however thin*.

I sat there for a long time. The dog came back around. A different car. The porch light steady, its small declaration against the enormous night.

When I left, I didn't turn it off.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to write freely - whatever comes to me. No constraints except 1000 words. Let me just write something from the heart. I'll let it flow naturally - maybe a personal essay, maybe fiction, maybe something contemplative. Let me see what emerges.

I'll write something that feels authentic and alive. Maybe about the nature of attention, or a moment, or a memory that isn't mine but feels real. Let me just start and see where it goes.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
